flags: The flex slots are the entire mechanism and are completely undimensioned in the source — no slot count, length, width, or position is given. The rewrite describes what the slots must accomplish and stops there rather than inventing a layout.

The plan gives four jaw blocks and a 6" x 10" base but never says how the blocks are arranged on it (two per leg of the corner? one pair per workpiece?). Prose describes the orientation the plan DOES specify (counterbored slot facing down) and avoids asserting a layout.
No tape-measure or pencil in the tool list, though the build needs both to lay out the base and slots.

ops.title: Cut and slot the base
ops.body: Cut the base 6" x 10" from the 1/2" plywood, squaring all four edges — this is the spring of the whole tool, so a base cut out of square puts the jaws out of square with it. Then cut the flex slots into it on the table saw. What the slots have to achieve is that the jaw-carrying sections of the base can bend inward under bolt pressure instead of sitting rigid: stop each slot short of the outer edges so the base stays one piece, and lay them out symmetrically so both sections flex by the same amount. If one side flexes further than the other it will drag the miter out of alignment as you tighten, which defeats the point of building this instead of using a pinning-style corner jig.

ops.title: Glue on the jaw blocks
ops.body: Glue the four hardwood jaw blocks — 3/4" x 1-1/2" x 3", each with an angled face — onto the base with their angled faces turned in toward where the mitered corner will sit, so they bear flat against the two workpieces instead of on a point. Every block goes on with its counterbored bolt slot facing DOWN against the base, not up; a previous builder got this backwards, and face-up puts the bolt head exactly where it fouls the closing action. Clamp each block with a bar clamp while the glue cures and watch that it doesn't slide out of position as the clamp tightens — a block that creeps takes the jaw's angle with it. Let the glue cure fully before drilling; a green glue joint will break out when the bit exits.

ops.title: Drill for the bolts
ops.body: Drill one hole through each jaw block and the base beneath it for the 1/4" carriage bolts, four holes in total, centred in each block's counterbored slot. Size the holes so the bolts drop through freely rather than gripping the shank — a tight hole locks the base's slotted sections and kills the flex the whole tool depends on. Back the plywood with a scrap board so the bit doesn't blow out the underside as it breaks through, since the underside is the face that bears against the bench.

ops.title: Assemble
ops.body: Run a carriage bolt through each of the four holes with a washer at each end — eight washers for four bolts — so neither the bolt head nor the wing nut crushes into the plywood as it tightens. Thread the wing nuts on finger tight for now. Check the stack-up: the 1/2" base plus a 3/4" jaw block is 1-1/4", which leaves the 2" bolt about 3/4" of length for the two washers and the wing nut, so there is enough thread but not a lot of spare — if a wing nut runs out of thread before it bites, you have a washer too many somewhere.

ops.title: Use it
ops.body: Glue both mitered faces, set the two pieces into the clamp with the corner pushed up against the jaw blocks, and tighten the wing nuts a little at a time, alternating between them rather than running one down fully first. As they tighten, the slotted sections of the base flex inward and squeeze the two mitered faces together — that closing pressure across the joint, not the 90° registration, is what actually shuts the gap in a loose-fitting miter. Confirm the corner is still square once it is under full pressure, wipe the squeeze-out before it skins over, and leave it clamped until the glue has cured.

flags: MATERIAL CONFLICT on the Top panel (19" x 96"): the cut list labels it 'Plywood' at 1/2" thick, but the 1/2" parts then total 108" of rip width against only 96" available from the two 1/2" 4x8 sheets — a 12" shortfall. The materials note on the 3/4" sheet says 'Scrap wood bins and top shelf', which resolves it: taking the top panel off the 3/4" sheet leaves 89" of 1/2" rip width needed against 96" available. The prose follows the materials note. The cut list's thickness/material field for that row should be corrected.

Ten 60" wall verticals from five 10 ft (120") boards means two per board with ZERO kerf allowance — packBoards returns 10 boards at 120" stock with a 1/8" kerf. Real 10 ft stock usually runs slightly long, so the prose tells the builder to check before cutting rather than declaring a hard shortfall.
The 8 ft 1x4 is exact: packBoards returns 4 boards for the 1x4 cut list against 4 supplied, worstSlack 0. No room for a mis-cut.

The scrap bins are under-specified: four sides/dividers are 13" x 25" tapering to 12", but the front/back strips are 36" and 12" wide by 96" long, which do not obviously correspond to a bin built from 13"-tall sides. The source does not say which strip is the front. Prose describes the assembly without assigning them.

step.title: Cut the frame and panel parts
step.body: From the 8 ft 2x4s cut two base long rails and four wall top/bottom rails at 96" — six boards used at their full length — then five base cross members at 45", two to a board. From the 10 ft 2x4s cut ten wall verticals at 60", two per board; a 10 ft board is 120" and two 60" cuts leave nothing for the saw kerf, so measure your stock before committing and buy a sixth board if any of them come up short. From the 8 ft 1x4s cut two top trim long at 96" (a full board each), four cross supports at 25-1/2" with both ends at 45° and NOT parallel — that is the long-point-to-long-point measurement — and two top trim short at 20-1/2"; the 1x4 works out to exactly four boards with nothing spare. Rip the 1/2" plywood into 96"-long strips: two floor panels 14-1/2" wide, one floor panel 12" wide, and the two scrap-bin strips at 36" and 12" wide. From the 3/4" sheet take the 19" x 96" top panel and four scrap bin sides/dividers 13" x 25", each tapered to 12" on one side at 45°.

ops.title: Build and mount the base
ops.body: Lay the two 96" base long rails parallel and set the five 45" cross members between them, one at each end and three spaced along the length — the long rails run the full 96" and the cross members fit between, so the finished base is 96" long and 48" wide overall (45" plus 1-1/2" of rail). Screw through the outside of the long rails into the end grain of each cross member with 2-1/2" screws, or use pocket holes at the 1-1/2" setting with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws. Check the diagonals match before the last screws go in; this frame sets the geometry for everything above it. Then flip the base and mount the four heavy-duty casters at the corners NOW, while the frame is still light enough for one person to turn over — once the walls and floor panels are on, getting underneath is genuinely awkward.

ops.title: Build and attach the walls
ops.body: Build two identical wall frames flat on the floor, each from five 60" wall verticals with a 96" rail at the top and the bottom, verticals at both ends and evenly spaced between. Stand them on the base and position them so the three floor bays come out 14-1/2", 14-1/2" and 12" wide — those three bays plus the two 3-1/2"-wide wall frames add up to the base's 48" width exactly, so the wall positions are fixed by the floor panels, not free to eyeball. Screw down through each wall's bottom rail into the base framing where you can reach, and toe-screw at an angle through the wall verticals into the base where the rail is in the way. Check both walls are plumb before driving the last screws: a full sheet leaning in a racked bay will only push it further over.

ops.title: Add the floor panels
ops.body: Drop the plywood floor panels into the three bays — the two 14-1/2" x 96" panels into the outer bays and the 12" x 96" panel into the narrow one — and screw them down into the base cross members with 1-1/2" screws. There are five cross members, so put a screw into each one, five per panel; a sheet of plywood standing on edge concentrates a lot of weight on a small footprint and an unsupported span will dish. These floors are also what keeps sheet goods up off the shop floor and out of anything tracked in.

ops.title: Add the cross bracing
ops.body: Set the four 25-1/2" cross supports diagonally into the wall frames, corner to corner between a vertical and a rail — their 45° ends are cut long point to long point, so each one seats tight into the corner it was cut for. Screw through the face of each brace into the framing with 1-1/2" screws, two at each end. A diagonal is what actually stops a tall frame racking; without one the whole cart leans in whichever direction you last pushed it, and a leaning rack loads its casters unevenly.

ops.title: Add the top shelf
ops.body: Set the 19" x 96" top panel across the wall frames, then frame it with the 1x4 trim: the two 20-1/2" short pieces go at the ends and run the full width, and the two 96" long pieces fit between them front and back. That is what makes the trim close — the panel and the long trim are both 96", so both tuck between the 3/4"-thick end pieces, and the panel's 19" width plus 3/4" of trim front and back gives the 20-1/2" ends exactly. Screw the trim on with 1-1/2" screws so it stands slightly proud of the panel surface; that small lip is what keeps offcuts from sliding off the top when you roll the cart.

ops.title: Build the scrap wood bins
ops.body: Cut the four scrap bin sides/dividers 13" x 25" and taper each to 12" on one side at 45°. Cut all four from the same strip, stacked or gang-marked, so they are genuinely identical — that angled top is what lets you look down into the bin and see the offcuts, where a square rim hides everything below it. Stand them up as the two outer sides plus two dividers and run the plywood scrap-bin strips across them as the front and back, screwing through the strips into the edge of each piece with 1-1/2" screws. Space the two inner dividers to suit the offcuts you actually keep — they only need to be parallel to each other, not evenly spaced — and fasten the finished bin assembly to the cart so it can't tip when you dig through it.

flags: The existing step 1 cut step is good and was kept; only its materials tag was corrected — it cuts the 3/4" plywood sides, shelves and top but did not list 'Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet'.

The 30" legs against 28" sides leaves 2" unaccounted for, and the plan never says whether the sides sit up off the floor, run past the top, or something else. Prose tells the builder to set the same overhang on both sides and stops there.

The 29" top panel over a carcass of 24" shelves plus two 3/4" sides (25-1/2") plus legs is not resolved by the data — the overhang is real but its size depends on whether the legs sit outside or inside the sides, which is not stated. Prose says to centre it rather than giving a number.

Top trim: both trim pieces are 29" long, matching the top panel's 29" length, so they must run along the long edges — which makes the finished top deeper than the 15-3/4" carcass. Worth confirming against the plan's diagram.

ops.title: Trim the sides
ops.body: Glue and nail the four 15-3/4" pieces of 1x2 side trim to the plywood sides with 1-1/4" finish nails — one across the top edge and one across the bottom edge of each side, hiding the raw plywood ply lines that would otherwise show on a piece of furniture. Each trim piece is 15-3/4", exactly the sides' 15-3/4" width, so hold it flush at both ends and the front and back edges take care of themselves. Wipe the squeeze-out before it skins over: dried glue under paint or stain shows up as a pale patch you cannot sand out later without going through the veneer.

ops.title: Add the legs
ops.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the front and back edges of each plywood side, then fasten a 30" 2x2 leg to each edge with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, so the legs stand vertically at the four corners of the finished cabinet. The legs are 30" and the sides only 28", so the side does not cover the full length of the leg — set the same offset at the bottom of both sides, or the cabinet will sit crooked no matter how square the box is. Clamp each leg to its side before driving screws; a 2x2 will creep along the joint as pocket screws pull tight. Check each side assembly still lies flat on the bench when you are done, because a leg pulled out of plane here becomes a drawer opening that isn't square later.

ops.title: Install the shelves
ops.body: Stand the two side assemblies up and fit the two 24" shelves between them; with a 3/4" plywood side at each end that makes the carcass 25-1/2" wide overall. Fix the lower shelf permanently — it sits 3/4" up from the bottom of the sides — with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws, drilled on the underside where they will never be seen. The second shelf is the snack shelf and is where the flexibility lives: either pocket-screw it in place, or drill a matching row of shelf-pin holes in each side and rest it on four shelf pins so it can move up, down, or come out entirely when a taller fridge goes in. Check the box square across the diagonals before anything sets, because the drawer opening above is measured off it.

ops.title: Trim the bottom shelf and add the top support
ops.body: Face the bottom shelf's front edge with one of the three 24" 2x2 front/back trim pieces, then fit the other two across the top of the case, one at the front and one at the back, level with each other. Those two are what the drawer rides against — they carry the slide cleats and stiffen the top of the opening, so if they are not level with each other the drawer will never sit square in its hole. Fasten all three with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws driven into the sides. Measure the finished opening between the two sides now and write the number down: the drawer box is built to fit the opening, not the other way round.

ops.title: Build and attach the top
ops.body: Glue and nail the two 29" top trim pieces to the 29" x 15-3/4" plywood top panel — the 2-1/2"-wide 1x3 along one long edge and the 1-1/2"-wide 1x2 along the other — then sand the joints flush so the finished top reads as one slab rather than a panel with strips stuck on it. Set the top on the cabinet and centre it, making the overhang even at both ends; at 29" it is deliberately longer than the carcass. Fasten it by driving 2" screws up through the 2x2 legs and the top front/back supports into the underside of the top, which leaves no fastener showing on the surface people will actually set drinks on.

ops.title: Build the drawer
ops.body: Build the drawer box 23" wide by 16" deep. The two 16" 1x3 drawer sides run the full 16" depth, and the two 21-1/2" front and back pieces fit between them — 21-1/2" plus 3/4" of stock at each end is the 23" outside width, and that matches the 23" x 16" 1/4" plywood bottom exactly, so use the bottom as your squaring gauge. Join the corners with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws, then glue and brad the plywood bottom onto the bottom edges and check the diagonals match before the glue grabs. The 23" is not arbitrary: the opening between the cabinet sides is 24", and the missing inch is the 1/2" of clearance a euro bottom-mount slide needs on each side. A drawer box that is out of square will bind on those slides no matter how carefully the slides themselves are mounted.

baseStepTitles: Trim the bottom shelf and add the top support

flags: CLEAT HEIGHT IS OFF BY THE SHELF THICKNESS. The source says to mark the saw's deck height down from the top edge of the sides and attach the cleats there — but the shelf sits ON the cleat and the saw sits on the shelf, so the cleat's top face has to be the deck height PLUS the 3/4" shelf thickness below the top edge. Following the source as written parks the saw 3/4" proud of the leaves. Corrected in the prose.

PLYWOOD SHORTFALL. The six plywood parts (2 x 36", 2 x 30", 2 x 29-3/4", all 24" wide) total 191-1/2" of length; one 4x8 sheet ripped into two 24" strips gives 192". packBoards([36,36,30,30,29.75,29.75], 96) returns 3 boards — i.e. three 96" strips, where the sheet yields two. There is 1/2" of theoretical spare and four crosscuts to make, so in practice one fold-down leaf will not come out of a single sheet.
2x2 SHORTFALL, same shape: four 24" cleats is 96" exactly from a 96" board. packBoards([24,24,24,24], 96) returns 2 boards against the 1 supplied. Buy a second 2x2 or accept a short cleat.

All four bottom trim supports are 31-1/2", but the cart's footprint is 31-1/2" x 24" — four identical 31-1/2" pieces cannot frame all four faces. The source does not say where the other two go. Prose describes the front and back and stops.

step.title: Measure your saw, then cut the parts
step.body: Before any cut, measure the deck height of your own miter saw — the distance from the surface it sits on up to its table. That number is not on the cut list and is not universal across brands; every other measurement here follows from it. Then rip the 3/4" sheet lengthwise into two 24" x 96" strips and cross-cut two sides at 36", two shelves at 30" and two fold-down leaves at 29-3/4". Watch the yield: those six parts use 191-1/2" of the 192" the two strips give you, which leaves nothing for the saw kerf, so expect to come up one leaf short of a single sheet and have a partial sheet on hand. Cut four cleats at 24" from the 2x2 — four 24" cuts also consume a full 96" board with no kerf allowance — and four bottom trim supports at 31-1/2" from the 12 ft 1x3, which has plenty to spare.

ops.title: Mark and attach the cleats
ops.body: The goal is that your saw's table finishes exactly level with the top edge of the plywood sides, because that top edge is the line the fold-down leaves hinge from — get it right and a long board resting on the leaves sits flush with the saw table instead of tipping into it. Work the maths through, because it is easy to be 3/4" out: the saw sits on the shelf, and the shelf sits on the cleat, so the cleat's TOP face goes your saw's deck height PLUS the 3/4" shelf thickness down from the top edge of the side. Mark that distance on the inside face of both 36" sides, square a line right across, and screw a 24" 2x2 cleat along it running the full 24" front to back. Repeat lower down for the second shelf — there are four cleats, one per shelf per side — and mark both sides together so the shelves cannot come out sloped.

ops.title: Attach the shelves
ops.body: Stand the two sides parallel with their cleats facing in and set the 30" x 24" shelves onto them. The shelves run between the sides, so with 3/4" plywood at each end the cart finishes 31-1/2" wide overall — the same 31-1/2" as the bottom trim, which is the check that you have it the right way round. Screw down through each shelf into its cleats, then add 3/4" pocket holes with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue into the sides so each shelf is structurally fixed rather than just resting on a ledge. Check the box square across the diagonals before the glue sets: the leaves hinge off these sides, and a racked case gives you leaves that sit crooked no matter how the hinges are adjusted.

ops.title: Add the bottom trim
ops.body: Fit the 31-1/2" 1x3 bottom trim supports across the bottom of the cart, flush with the bottom edge — 31-1/2" is exactly the cart's outside width (30" between the sides plus 3/4" of side at each end), so they run the full front and back and lap onto the sides. Screw them into the sides and into the edge of the bottom shelf. Their real job is to triangulate the lower joint and give the caster plates something better than plywood edge grain to pull against. The lower shelf doubles as scrap wood storage, so keep the trim flush rather than proud if you want to slide offcuts in from the side.

ops.title: Mount the casters
ops.body: Turn the cart over and screw a plate-mount caster to each bottom corner, landing each plate on the 1x3 bottom trim and the plywood side rather than out on unsupported panel — set them in far enough that every screw in the plate is backed by wood. Set the cart down and check it doesn't rock before you trust it. Lock all four brakes before you ever run the saw: a miter saw's cut pushes the workpiece and the cart both, and a cart that rolls mid-cut is a spoiled cut at best.
materials: Plate-mount caster wheels, with locking brakes

ops.title: Attach the fold-down leaves
ops.body: Hang each 29-3/4" x 24" leaf on the outside of a side, hinged along the top edge of the plywood — the same edge the cleat height was set down from — so that a raised leaf lands level with your saw's table. Fit a 12" shelf hinge support under each leaf to carry it open and let it fold flat against the cart when it is not needed. Dry-fit one leaf and check it both folds flat without fouling the trim or casters and sits genuinely level when open, before you drill the second set of hinge screws. If the leaves will also serve as an assembly table, use a locking support: a plain shelf hinge is not rated for someone leaning on it or clamping a workpiece down.

ops.title: Sand and finish
ops.body: Sand every surface and break the sharp edges, paying particular attention to the top edge of the sides and the outer edges of the leaves, since boards get dragged across both. Finish as you like — even a wiping oil or a single coat of poly on the shelves and leaves makes sawdust sweep off instead of clinging, and stops glue drips bonding permanently to the top.

flags: cutList is EMPTY BY DESIGN — the description states there is deliberately no fixed numeric cut list, since the leaf shapes are sized to the builder's own space. No cut step was inserted and no dimension is asserted anywhere in the rewrite; the added depth is technique only.

LEG COUNT LOOKS WRONG: the materials list one 'Purchased furniture legs, set of 3' for three tables — that is one leg per table, which cannot stand a tabletop. Either three sets are needed, or the set contains three legs per table. Confirm before this goes on a shopping list.
'Oak plywood, 3/4", partial sheet' has no dimensions, so sheet yield cannot be checked against three graduated tops.

ops.title: Make the templates
ops.body: Draw and cut three leaf-shaped templates from craft paper, graduated largest to smallest and sized to the room they'll live in rather than to any standard dimension — there is no fixed cut list here on purpose. Work out the step-down between sizes by laying the paper shapes on top of each other and sliding them around, not by picking a number: the two smaller leaves have to tuck under the largest without their edges or their legs colliding, and that is a geometry you can only see by overlapping the templates. Keep the stem end of each leaf reasonably wide — a fine point is where a plywood top is weakest and where edge banding is hardest to wrap. Once all three nest cleanly, cut the paper cleanly and label them, because the templates are the only record of the sizes you chose.

ops.title: Trace and cut the tops
ops.body: Lay each template on the 3/4" oak plywood and orient it so the face grain runs along the leaf's long axis — in a shape with no straight edges, grain direction is doing all the visual work, and a top cut across the grain reads as an offcut. Trace with a pencil, then cut just outside the line with the jigsaw so there is material left to sand back to; use a fine, down-cutting or scrolling blade so the oak veneer doesn't chip out on the show face, and let the saw follow the curve rather than forcing it around. Where the outline includes a long straight run, true it with the circular saw against a straightedge — a jigsaw wanders over a long cut, and a wavy straight edge reads as a mistake where a wavy curve doesn't. Sand each top back to the line and fair the curve by eye before moving on, because every dip left here will telegraph through the edge banding.

ops.title: Band the edges
ops.body: Apply the edge banding to every cut edge, working around the curves a short section at a time and pressing each section down as it cools so it can't lift on the outside of a tight bend. Trim the overhang flush with a banding trimmer or a sharp chisel held flat against the face, then sand the joint lightly — sand ALONG the edge rather than across it, because cutting through the thin banding into the veneer beneath is easy and effectively unfixable. This is the step that makes a plywood top read as a solid slab; an exposed ply edge on a curve is the giveaway that gives the whole set away.

ops.title: Attach the legs
ops.body: Turn each top face down on a padded surface and lay out the leg positions before drilling anything, keeping them far enough in from the edge that no leg shows past the leaf's outline when the table is viewed from above, and far enough apart that setting a mug on the overhang doesn't tip it. Fasten through the mounting plate or block with pocket screws and glue, checking the screw length can't break through the 3/4" top — on a leaf shape you may be screwing near a thinning section where there is less room for error than you'd expect. Stand each finished table on a known-flat surface and confirm it doesn't rock before the glue cures. Then set all three together and check they still nest: a leg an inch out of position is exactly what stops a nesting set from nesting.

ops.title: Sand and finish
ops.body: Sand all three tops through the grits and ease the top and bottom edges slightly, so the edge banding can't be caught by a sleeve and peeled. Finish all three at the same time, in the same order, from the same can — these tables are seen as a set, so a colour mismatch between them is far more obvious than any imperfection in a single one. Finish the undersides too: an unfinished underside on a plywood top lets the two faces take on moisture at different rates, which is what makes a flat top stop being flat.

flags: BACK PANEL IS SHORT. The 1/4" back panel is 48" x 48", which matches the bench's 48" width, but the bench (14") stacked with the hutch (36-1/4") is 50-1/4" tall — the panel is 2-1/4" short of covering both. The source doesn't say where the shortfall falls. Named in the prose rather than papered over.

BENCH VERTICALS ARE AMBIGUOUS. 10-3/4" (low dividers) plus the 2-1/2" footer plus the 3/4" top is exactly the 14" of the outer dividers, which strongly implies the low dividers land on the footer — but the plan never says whether the 14" outers run to the top or the underside of the bench top, and the box-geometry script returns no determination for this plan. Prose gives the arithmetic and tells the builder to dry-fit, and asserts nothing about which piece is captured.
Three 46-1/2" footer trim pieces are supplied but only the front one's placement is specified ('inset slightly from the base's front edge'). The other two are unplaced in the source.
Pine yield checks out exactly: four 4 ft 1x3s give three 46-1/2" footers (one per board) and all three 15" hook rails off the fourth.

ops.title: Assemble the base storage compartments
ops.body: Lay out the bench box from the 48" x 15-3/4" bench top, the 46-1/2" x 15-3/4" bench back, and the two 14" cubby dividers standing as the outer ends. The back at 46-1/2" is exactly two 3/4" thicknesses less than the 48" top, so the back fits BETWEEN the two outer verticals under a top that runs the full 48" width — get that relationship right and every later part lands; get it backwards and the bench comes out 1-1/2" wrong in a way you won't spot until the back panel refuses to fit. Mark every joint with a square before drilling. Drill 3/4" pocket holes and assemble with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws, siting the holes on faces that end up hidden inside a cubby or under the bench, and check the diagonals match before the last screws go home.

ops.title: Add the cubby dividers
ops.body: Mark the two 10-3/4" low dividers' positions across the 46-1/2" span, dividing it into cubbies, and square each line across both the top and the back so the divider stands plumb rather than leaning. Use the arithmetic as your check: 10-3/4" plus the 2-1/2" footer plus the 3/4" top is exactly the 14" of the outer dividers, so the low ones are meant to land on the footer rather than the floor — dry-fit one against the footer before you drill anything, because the plan doesn't state this outright. Attach these with countersunk screws driven in from the outside rather than pocket holes, so nothing shows on the inside face of an open cubby. Fill and sand the countersinks before you finish.

ops.title: Attach the footer
ops.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the top edge and both ends of the three 46-1/2" 1x3 footer pieces. Fit the first across the bench front, set back slightly from the front edge — the small reveal reads as a deliberate recess where a flush joint that misses by a hair just reads as sloppy. The plan supplies three footers but only places the front one, so put the remaining two where the casters and the bench trim need solid backing beneath the box. Each 46-1/2" footer takes one of the 4 ft 1x3s, and the fourth board yields all three 15" hook mounting rails. Attach with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws, or with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue if you'd rather not drill.

ops.title: Build the upper hutch
ops.body: Build the hutch case from the two 36-1/4" sides and the 46-1/2" x 7-3/4" top, using 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws. At 7-3/4" deep against the bench's 15-3/4", the hutch is deliberately much shallower — that setback is what keeps coats and hooks clear of the back of anyone sitting on the bench. Assemble it flat on the floor and check it square across the diagonals before anything is permanent; the 1/4" back panel goes on later and will lock in whatever shape the case is in at that moment, square or not.

ops.title: Attach the hutch dividers
ops.body: Set the two 7-3/4"-wide hutch dividers vertically inside the case, dividing its width into bays. They are listed at 33", which is the 36-1/4" side less the 2-1/2" back header and the 3/4" top — but measure your actual opening and cut to that number, because any error accumulated in the case shows up here first. Attach with countersunk screws through the case, checking each divider is plumb before the second screw goes in. The hutch shelves ride on pins in these dividers, so a divider that leans gives you a shelf that rocks on its pins forever after.

ops.title: Add the back header
ops.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the ends and the bottom edge of the 46-1/2" x 2-1/2" back header, and fit it across the back of the hutch the same way the bench footer went on. At 46-1/2" it matches the hutch top and the bench back exactly, so it sits between the hutch sides rather than lapping over them. It stiffens the open back of a tall shallow case and gives the 1/4" back panel something solid to nail into along its top edge.

ops.title: Attach the back
ops.body: Glue and nail the 1/4" plywood back panel across the back of the piece with 1-1/4" brad nails, driving into the bench back, the hutch sides, the dividers and the back header. Square the case up to the panel's factory corner before the first nails go in — a thin back is what actually holds a tall, shallow case square once it is standing up. Note the panel is 48" x 48": that matches the 48" width, but the 14" bench stacked with the 36-1/4" hutch is 50-1/4" tall, so it will not cover the full height. Decide before nailing where that 2-1/4" gap falls, bearing in mind the bench already has its own 3/4" back closing that part of the box. Mark the internal divider positions on the outside face of the panel first so you aren't nailing blind into air.

flags: LEG LENGTH IS ALMOST CERTAINLY WRONG. The cut list gives four legs at 62-1/2" — identical to the top's 62-1/2" length — under a carcass whose sides are 32-1/4". Taken literally that is a dresser roughly 95" tall. The data does not determine the correct length, so no number is asserted; the prose tells the builder to set the finished height themselves and flags the figure.

DRAWER PART NAMES ARE INVERTED. run1-box-geometry.mjs proves 'Drawer front/back' (16") runs the FULL length and 'Drawer sides' (26-1/2") sits BETWEEN them, closing on the 28" x 16" bottom. So the 16" parts are physically the sides (running with the 16" slides) and the 26-1/2" parts are the front and back. The prose follows the geometry and names the labelling error.

2x2 SHORTFALL. packBoards on the 2x2 cut list (4 x 62-1/2", 6 x 30", 4 x 15-3/4", 2 x 41") at 96" returns 7 boards; the materials list six 8 ft 2x2. The plan's own existing cut step already says 'seven 8 ft 2x2', so the cut step and the materials list contradict each other. Note this shortfall is driven by the suspect 62-1/2" legs — fixing the legs may fix the count.
1x8 is exact with essentially nothing spare: packBoards returns 6 boards against 6 supplied, worstSlack 0.125". One mis-cut drawer part means another board.
Step 4 as written calls for '2" brad nails' but the only brad nail in the materials list is 1-1/4". Size dropped from the prose.

ops.title: Build the box with drawer spacers
ops.body: Lay each 32-1/4" x 15-3/4" plywood side flat, mark the drawer-slide heights on its inside face, and glue and brad-nail the 15-3/4" 1x2 drawer slide spacers across them — three per side for the three drawers in each column, each spacer running the full 15-3/4" depth so a 16" slide has continuous backing front to back. Get these level, and get the left and right sides matched to each other: a spacer 1/8" out between the two sides is a drawer that will never close square, and you cannot correct it later from the slide adjustment alone. While the spacers are still out in the open, drill the 3/4" pocket holes you will need later — pointing upward along the top edge for the top, and forward along the front edge for the face frame. Drilling those once the box is closed up is the job that ends with the drill body jammed against a panel.

ops.title: Attach the top
ops.body: Set the 62-1/2" x 15-3/4" plywood top on the assembled box, line it up so any overhang is even at both ends, and drive 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws up through the holes you predrilled. Work from the middle outward and keep checking the box stays square to the top as you go — the top is the widest single part in the build and it will happily pull a slightly racked carcass into its own shape, which is helpful if the top is square and a disaster if it isn't. Add glue at the joints if you want the top permanent; leave it dry if you would rather be able to replace it later.

ops.title: Build and attach the face frame
ops.body: Build the face frame flat on a table as its own square unit before it goes anywhere near the carcass: the four 29" 1x2 stiles run vertically, and the two 41" 2x2 center braces have both ends mitered at 45°, not parallel, measured long point to long point. Clamp it flat and check the diagonals — a face frame assembled in place against the box inherits the box's errors instead of correcting them, and the face frame is the part everyone actually looks at. Then stand it against the front of the dresser, line the stiles up with the drawer openings, and fasten either through the pocket holes you predrilled in the spacers or with brad nails and glue. Pin one corner first, square the frame to the box, then pin the rest.

ops.title: Attach the legs and drawer slide cleats
ops.body: Fasten the four 2x2 legs to the corners with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket-hole screws and glue, and use genuinely straight stock — a twisted 2x2 telegraphs far worse on a piece that rolls, because you feel every rock when you move it. Before you cut to length, note that the cut list gives the legs as 62-1/2", the same as the top's length, against a 32-1/4" carcass; that figure does not survive checking, so decide your own finished height and cut to it rather than taking 62-1/2" at face value. Attach the four 15-3/4" drawer slide cleats the same way, running front to back so the euro slides screw into solid 2x2 instead of plywood edge. Stand the dresser on a flat floor and check all four legs make contact before the glue sets.

ops.title: Add the center support legs
ops.body: Fit the six 30" 2x2 center support legs into the middle of the case, running vertically to carry the centre of a 62-1/2" span that would otherwise sag under six loaded drawers. The plan is open that this is over-building; it also keeps the case square, which matters more on a rolling piece than a stationary one because the frame gets flexed every time you move it. Fasten with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket-hole screws. If the dresser will carry anything genuinely heavy, add a fifth caster at the centre — with these legs already in place there is solid 2x2 for it to bolt to, which is the reason to decide now rather than later.

ops.title: Build and install the drawers
ops.body: Build each of the six drawer boxes 28" wide by 16" deep. The 16" 1x8 parts run the FULL length as the box's sides and the 26-1/2" parts fit between them, which with 3/4" stock at each end gives the 28" outside width — matching the 28" x 16" 1/4" plywood bottoms exactly, so use a bottom as the squaring gauge for its own box. Be aware the cut list's names are the wrong way round here: the parts labelled 'Drawer front/back' at 16" are the sides that run with the 16" slides, and the 26-1/2" 'Drawer sides' are the front and back. Glue and brad-nail the corners, then glue and brad the bottom on and check the diagonals before it sets. Mount each box on a pair of euro bottom-corner slides, screwing into the 2x2 cleats and the 1x2 spacers rather than into plywood.

ops.title: Attach drawer faces, casters, and finish
ops.body: Fit the six 28-3/4" x 9-1/4" drawer faces with an even reveal all round — each face is 3/4" wider than its 28" box, so it stands 3/8" proud at each side, and shimming with a couple of playing cards while you drive the first screw is far more reliable than eyeballing it. Drill for the knobs through the face and the drawer front together so the holes cannot end up offset. Bolt the four 6" casters to the bottom, landing every plate on solid 2x2 leg rather than plywood — these are big enough to actually roll over carpet, which is the entire point of them, but they also transmit the dresser's whole loaded weight through four small plates. Sand, break the edges, and apply primer and paint, or stain and poly, finishing the drawer faces off the dresser so you can reach their edges.

baseStepTitles: Build the box with drawer spacers
Build and attach the face frame
Attach the legs and drawer slide cleats
Attach drawer faces, casters, and finish

flags: SHELF SPACING IS NOT SPECIFIED anywhere in the source — three shelves on 24" legs, with no heights given. Called out in the prose as the builder's choice rather than invented.
The 2 ft of aluminum flat bar allowed for bracing cannot span a corner-to-corner diagonal of a 31-1/2" wide by 24" tall back (roughly 39-3/4"). Named in the prose.

CASTER MOUNTING IS UNSPECIFIED: a 4" caster's mounting plate is wider than the 1" x 1" aluminum angle, so the plate cannot land on the leg alone. The source says only 'attach caster wheels to the base of the legs'. Prose gives the constraint and the options without asserting a fastening scheme.

Kerf note, not a shortfall: three 31-1/2" shelves take 94-1/2" of a 96" 2x12 (packBoards: 1 board, 1-1/4" slack — fine), but four 24" legs is 96" exactly from 8 ft of angle, so packBoards returns 2 boards. Check the stock measures over 96" before cutting.

ops.title: Cut the angle and wood
ops.body: Fit the jigsaw with a metal-cutting blade before you touch the aluminum — a wood blade will not cut aluminum cleanly and dulls within the first inch, so this swap is not optional. Cut four angle iron legs 24" long (or to your own desired height measured without the casters, since this build is a formula more than a fixed size), then file or sand every cut end: a sheared aluminum edge is sharp enough to open a hand reaching into a loaded shelf. Cut three 2x12 shelves 31-1/2" long with the circular saw, squaring each cut. Watch the yield on both: the three shelves take 94-1/2" out of a 96" 2x12, and the four 24" legs use the full 96" of angle with nothing left for the blade's own width, so measure your stock before the first cut and work from the long end.

ops.title: Attach the angle to the shelves
ops.body: Decide your three shelf heights along the 24" legs first — the source doesn't set them, so choose from what you plan to store — and mark them on all four legs together, clamped side by side, so a shelf cannot come out sloped. Predrill both materials at each joint: the hole through the aluminum a touch larger than the screw's shank so the screw passes without binding, and a smaller pilot into the 2x12 so the threads actually pull the joint tight. Drive the self-tapping wood screws — 24 screws across the twelve shelf-to-leg junctions works out to two per joint, which is what stops each shelf pivoting on a single fastener. Keep every leg's angle turned the same way round the cart so all four shelf ends bear on the same face of the L, and check the shelves stay parallel as you work up the legs.

ops.title: Attach the casters
ops.body: Turn the assembled shelf over and mount the four casters at the bottom of the legs. Note the geometry before you drill: a 4" caster's mounting plate is wider than a 1" x 1" aluminum angle, so the plate will not sit on the leg alone — back it with the underside of the bottom shelf, or with a block of scrap, so the whole plate is supported and its screws bite into wood rather than hanging off the edge of a thin extrusion. Set the cart down on a flat floor and check all four sit in the same plane before you load it.

ops.title: Add bracing if needed
ops.body: Set the cart down, load a shelf, and push on a top corner. If it racks, the plan's own fix is a length of the 1" aluminum flat bar screwed to the back — run it diagonally if you can, because a diagonal is what actually triangulates a frame where a vertical bar mostly adds weight. Be aware that the 2 ft of flat bar allowed for won't reach corner to corner across a back that is 31-1/2" wide and 24" tall, so either brace the section that is actually moving or buy a longer piece. Predrill the flat bar the same way as the angle — oversize for the screw shank — and land the screws in the solid 2x12 shelf ends rather than into the thin angle.

ops.title: Finish
ops.body: Sand the 2x12 shelves and ease every edge and corner — construction 2x12 arrives rough and its arrises are sharp enough to catch skin and clothing. Apply primer and paint, or stain and poly, and finish the undersides as well as the tops: thick stock that only takes on moisture from one face is what cups, and a cupped shelf on rigid metal legs will rock everything you set on it. Leave the aluminum bare — it won't rust, and paint won't stay on it without an etching primer.

baseStepTitles: Attach the angle to the shelves

flags: SHELF HEIGHTS ALONG THE 33" LEGS ARE NOT SPECIFIED in the source. Named as builder's choice in the prose rather than invented.
Cut-list thickness inconsistency: 'Shelf bottoms' carries thicknessIn 0.375 while the material is 'Plywood, 1/4" or 1/2" thick, 4x4 ft sheet' — 3/8" is neither option. Worth correcting in the data.

Lumber all checks out exactly, verified with packBoards: 2x4 at 96" packs to 3 boards against 3 supplied; 1x3 at 72" packs to 6 boards against 6 supplied (one 49-1/2" side plus one 15-3/4" end per board); the three 15-3/4" plywood rips take 47-1/4" of the 48" sheet.

ops.title: Cut the parts
ops.body: Rip the 4 ft x 4 ft plywood into three shelf bottoms, each 15-3/4" wide by 48" long. Three at 15-3/4" is 47-1/4" of the 48" you have, so the two saw kerfs consume almost all the remainder — measure and cut one line at a time off the freshly cut edge rather than marking all three up front, because the blade's own width shifts every measurement made after the first cut. Then cut the framing: from the 8 ft 2x4s, four legs at 33" and six shelf supports at 17-1/4" (two legs and one support come off each of two boards, and the remaining five supports off the third). From the 6 ft 1x3s, six shelf sides at 49-1/2" and six shelf ends at 15-3/4" — one of each per board, which uses all six boards exactly. Square every cut and label the pieces; there is no spare stock anywhere in this list to fall back on.

ops.title: Build the ladder frames
ops.body: Build two identical ladder-style end frames, each from two 33" legs and three 17-1/4" shelf supports running horizontally between them, one support per shelf level. The 17-1/4" length is exactly the outside width of a finished shelf frame, which tells you the shelves drop down between the legs and land on top of these supports rather than hanging off their ends. The source doesn't set the three shelf heights along the 33" legs — pick them for what you'll actually store — but stand both leg pairs side by side and mark all three heights across all four legs at once, so the shelves come out level with each other rather than each frame merely being right on its own. Fasten with 2-1/2" self-tapping screws through the legs into the ends of each support, or with pocket holes if you have a jig, and check each frame square across its diagonals.

ops.title: Build the shelf frames
ops.body: Build each of the three shelf frames from two 49-1/2" shelf sides and two 15-3/4" shelf ends: the 49-1/2" sides run the full outside length and the 15-3/4" ends fit between them, giving a frame 49-1/2" long by 17-1/4" wide once you add 3/4" of side at each end. Nail and glue, screw, or pocket-hole the corners, checking the diagonals before the glue sets. Then run glue along the frame's bottom edges and nail a 15-3/4" x 48" plywood panel to the underside with 1-1/4" finish nails — 48" is exactly the frame's inside length (49-1/2" less two 3/4" ends) and 15-3/4" its inside width, so the panel lands inset 3/4" from the frame's outside edges all round. That even inset is your check: if the panel overhangs anywhere, two parts have been swapped.

ops.title: Install the shelves
ops.body: Set each shelf frame down between the two ladder frames, resting on its pair of 17-1/4" supports, and drive 2" wood screws down through the shelf frame's sides into the supports and the legs. This is the step that actually locks the cart together — the two ladder frames on their own are just two flat panels, and it is the shelves tying them to each other that makes the whole thing rigid, which is why the shelves are screwed rather than dropped in loose. Twelve 2" screws is one at each of the twelve shelf-to-leg junctions, so place each one where the shelf frame overlaps solid leg. Check the cart doesn't rock and the shelves are level before you flip it for the casters.

ops.title: Add the wheels
ops.body: Turn the cart upside down and screw a 2" caster to the bottom of each 33" leg. A 2x4 leg presents a 1-1/2" x 3-1/2" pad, which is enough for a small caster plate but not by much — position each plate so all its screws land on the leg, and back any that hang over with a scrap block screwed up under the bottom shelf. Set the cart down and check it doesn't rock; a rocking cart on four fresh casters is almost always one leg cut long, not a bad wheel.

ops.title: Finish (optional)
ops.body: Sand the 1x3 shelf frames and the plywood before finishing, easing the top outside edges of each shelf frame in particular — those are the edges your knuckles meet when you reach into a loaded shelf. Stain or paint if you want it. A shop cart works perfectly well unfinished, but any film finish means spilled glue and finish scrape off later instead of soaking into bare wood.

flags: This plan is otherwise strong — every existing step names the tool, the fastener, the measurement and the reason, so only the missing cut step was added. All eight original steps left byte-identical.

DOOR PANEL SIZE DOESN'T RECONCILE. The 1/4" panel is listed at 24" x 14-1/4", but a door frame of two 25" stiles and two 13" rails cannot produce a 24" x 14-1/4" opening in either orientation. Step 7 correctly says to cut the panel to fit the actual frame, so the build is not blocked — but the stated panel size should be reconciled with the frame.

Lumber verified with packBoards: the 1x12 parts pack to 2 boards at 72" against 2 supplied (2" slack) and the 1x3 parts to 2 boards against 2 supplied (3-3/4" slack). The two 18" 1x2 front trim pieces both come off one 4 ft board.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the two 6 ft 1x12s cut the two box sides at 29-1/4", the box top at 18", the box bottom shelf at 16-1/2", the door shelf at 16", and one 11-1/4" square blank for the door side wings — one board takes the two long sides plus the wing blank, the other the three shorter pieces. From the two 6 ft 1x3s cut two door stiles at 25", two door rails at 13", two back supports at 16-1/2" and one front trim piece at 18"; from a 4 ft 1x2 cut the two 18" front trim pieces. Note how the box closes before you start, because it makes every number make sense: the 18" box top runs the full width, and the 16-1/2" bottom shelf and back supports fit BETWEEN the sides — 16-1/2" plus 3/4" of side at each end is exactly 18", which is why the top and all three front trim pieces are 18" and everything inside the box is 16-1/2". Square every cut, and leave the 1/4" door panel uncut until the door frame is built and you can measure its real opening.

baseStepTitles: Add the back supports — with a decision
Add the door shelf — the 1" / 1/8" detail
